steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks why the finding about microorganism community composition mattered. The study transplanted plant-soil cores to a warmer elevation and observed accelerated nutrient cycling. The key detail is that the microorganism community composition was unchanged after transplanting.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — It provided preliminary evidence that microorganism-mediated nutrient cycling was accelerated in the transplanted cores.: The acceleration was directly observed, not inferred from composition data. This misidentifies what the composition finding contributed. ✗
  • Choice B — It suggested that temperature-induced changes in microorganism activity may be occurring at increasingly high elevations.: The study involved transplanting to a lower elevation, and the composition finding says nothing about a broader geographic trend. ✗
  • Choice C — It ruled out a potential alternative explanation for the acceleration in microorganism-mediated nutrient cycling.: If the microbial community had changed at the new elevation, that shift could have explained the acceleration. By confirming composition stayed the same, researchers could attribute the acceleration to temperature alone — eliminating an alternative explanation. ✓
  • Choice D — It clarified that microorganism activity levels in the plant-soil cores varied depending on which microorganisms comprised the community.: This inverts the finding. The community composition did not change, so it could not explain variation in activity levels. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
